name: Quadrantids
description: One of the best annual meteor showers, producing up to 120 meteors per hour at peak. Active for only a few hours, making timing critical.
viewingTips: Best viewed from northern latitudes. Look northeast from midnight to dawn. Dress warmly — January nights are cold!

historicalStorms.notes: Exceptional outburst, one of the strongest Quadrantid peaks in recent years

historicalVisibility: Northern Hemisphere circumpolar; poor from southern latitudes

name: Lyrids
description: One of the oldest recorded meteor showers, with observations dating back over 2,700 years. Produces bright, fast meteors with occasional fireballs.
viewingTips: Look toward Vega (in Lyra) in the northeast sky. Best after midnight. May produce unexpected outbursts.

historicalStorms.notes: Spectacular storm observed over the eastern United States
One of the most intense Lyrid storms on record

historicalVisibility: Visible from both hemispheres; better from northern latitudes

name: Eta Aquariids
description: Debris from Halley's Comet. Southern hemisphere observers can see rates of 50+ per hour. Known for very fast meteors with persistent trains.
viewingTips: Best from southern latitudes before dawn. In the north, expect 10-30 meteors/hour near the horizon.

historicalStorms.notes: Significant outburst; widely observed from Australia

historicalVisibility: Southern Hemisphere strongly favoured; northern visibility limited

name: Delta Aquariids
description: A moderate shower that overlaps with the start of Perseid season. Produces faint meteors with no persistent trains.
viewingTips: Look toward Aquarius in the south. Works well combined with Perseid watching in late July/early August.
historicalVisibility: Best from southern latitudes; visible (but reduced) from the north

name: Perseids
description: The most popular meteor shower of the year. Warm summer nights and high ZHR make it perfect for casual observers. Produces many bright fireballs.
viewingTips: No equipment needed. Find a dark spot, lie back, and look up. Best from midnight to 4am. Allow 20 minutes for eyes to dark-adapt.

historicalStorms.notes: Major outburst predicted and confirmed by Donald Yeomans
Enhanced activity due to Jupiter gravitational effects on debris trail

historicalVisibility: Northern Hemisphere prime target; radiant circumpolar above 40°N

name: Orionids
description: The second annual Halley's Comet shower. Very fast meteors often leave glowing persistent trains lasting several seconds.
viewingTips: Look above and left of Orion's famous Belt stars. Best after midnight when Orion is high.

name: Leonids
description: Famous for historic meteor storms. 2026 is not a storm year, but Leonids are among the fastest meteors reaching Earth — entry velocity of 71 km/s.
viewingTips: Meteors appear to radiate from the Sickle of Leo in the east. Dark skies essential as rates are low in non-storm years.

historicalStorms.notes: The Great Meteor Storm — up to 100,000 per hour, changed astronomy
The most intense meteor storm of the 20th century
Modern storm, widely observed across Europe and Asia
Two-peak storm in North America and Asia

historicalVisibility: Northern Hemisphere favoured; radiant rises after midnight

name: Geminids
description: The king of meteor showers. Unusually sourced from an asteroid, the Geminids are rich, slow, and colourful — including white, yellow, and green meteors.
viewingTips: The radiant rises before midnight, making this an all-night shower. Rates build through the evening. Slow meteors (35 km/s) are easier to spot.

historicalStorms.notes: Exceptional year with above-average rates observed worldwide
Favourable peak; widely shared on social media

historicalVisibility: Visible from both hemispheres; best from north due to radiant declination

name: Ursids
description: A quiet end-of-year shower. The radiant is circumpolar in the Northern Hemisphere, meaning it never sets. Can produce unexpected outbursts.
viewingTips: Look toward Polaris (the North Star). Best observed in the pre-dawn hours. Cold but peaceful winter sky.
historicalVisibility: Northern Hemisphere only; invisible from south of equator
